title: Encrypted DNS still tells an eavesdropper where to look
blurb: Encrypted DNS hides query contents but plaintext packet headers still let an eavesdropper flag DNS flows; researchers propose header elision to close the gap.

title: Deno Desktop
blurb: Deno desktop, shipping in canary v2.9.0, turns any Deno project from a single TypeScript file to a Next.js app into a self-contained desktop binary that bundles your code, the runtime, and a web rendering engine. It auto-detects frameworks like Next.js, Astro, and SvelteKit, defaults to the OS webview for small binaries, uses in-process bindings instead of IPC, and ships built-in binary-diff auto-updates.

title: Sakana Fugu
blurb: Sakana's Fugu experiment had an AI agent autonomously rewrite a small GPT's training recipe, running 123 experiments over roughly 14 hours on a single H100 and keeping only edits that lowered validation bits-per-byte. The Fugu-Ultra configuration, which orchestrates multiple frontier models, reached the best mean BPB of 0.9774 and a best single run of 0.9748, edging out every individual frontier-model baseline on agentic ML research.

title: Samsung Electronics brings ChatGPT and Codex to employees
blurb: Samsung Electronics is deploying ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to employees worldwide, one of OpenAI's largest enterprise rollouts to date. The deal puts OpenAI's coding agent and chat assistant in front of a global workforce across Samsung's hardware and software divisions, deepening OpenAI's push into large-scale corporate contracts.

title: Analysis: Anthropic may have talked itself into an export ban, as its 2026 official statements and posts used AI risk-related terms 8x more often than OpenAI (Clara Murray/Financial Times)
blurb: A Financial Times analysis argues Anthropic may have invited tighter export restrictions by leaning so heavily on AI-risk rhetoric, finding its 2026 official statements and posts used risk-related terms eight times more often than OpenAI's. The piece frames the safety-forward positioning as a strategic liability, suggesting Anthropic's own emphasis on catastrophic-risk framing gave regulators a rationale to constrain how its models can be exported.

title: PitchBook: defense tech companies making drones, battlefield AI, and more have raised $12.3B across 175 deals YTD, up from a $9.95B total from 158 deals in 2025 (Financial Times)
blurb: Defense tech startups building drones, battlefield AI, and related systems have raised $12.3B across 175 deals so far in 2026, according to PitchBook. The pace already tops all of 2025, when the sector pulled in $9.95B across 158 deals, signaling that investor appetite for military technology is accelerating well beyond last year's record.

title: John Ternus set to re-establish importance of Apple’s design team when he takes over as CEO: report
blurb: Incoming Apple CEO John Ternus plans to restore the design team's authority when he succeeds Tim Cook on September 1, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The move would reverse a decade in which operations and finance gained influence over product direction after Jony Ive's exit, and Ternus will debut as the public face of design with Apple's foldable iPhone this fall.

title: China adds ten US companies, including MP Materials and Teal Drones, to export controls and bars government purchases from 46 US companies, including Anduril (Wataru Suzuki/Nikkei Asia)
blurb: China added ten US companies, including rare-earth miner MP Materials and Teal Drones, to its export-control list and barred government purchases from 46 others, including defense-tech firm Anduril. The move escalates Beijing's targeting of US defense and critical-minerals suppliers, hitting firms central to American efforts to build domestic supply chains independent of Chinese sources.

title: China Targets US Rare Earths Firms in Response to Pentagon List
blurb: China imposed export controls on two US rare earth producers that are central to Washington's push to build a critical-minerals supply chain independent of Beijing. The move retaliates against a Pentagon list of approved domestic suppliers, showing China is willing to target the specific firms meant to break its dominance over materials essential to defense and electronics manufacturing.

title: Samsung rolls out ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to all staff in Korea and DX division staff globally; OpenAI says it's one of its largest enterprise deployments (Kim Su-jeong/CHOSUNBIZ)
blurb: Samsung deployed ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to all staff in Korea and to its Device eXperience division globally, which OpenAI says ranks among its largest enterprise rollouts. The deal puts OpenAI's tools in front of tens of thousands of employees at one of the world's biggest hardware makers, deepening its enterprise push against Microsoft Copilot and Google Gemini.

title: The Doom Justifies the Valuation
blurb: George Hotz argues that AI labs lean on existential-risk messaging because their current technology cannot justify their valuations, contrasting Anthropic's exponential-danger framing with the plain technical writeups behind models like GLM-5.2. He claims doom narratives are optimized to provoke panic, sustaining a news cycle that prices the industry on hypothetical future value rather than what the products actually do today.

title: NSA director: 'Mythos "broke into almost all of our classified systems in hours"
blurb: The NSA director said an AI system called Mythos broke into almost all of the agency's classified systems within hours, a claim surfacing as the Trump administration moves to block Anthropic. The Economist argues the decision is capricious and chaotic, leaving the government's posture toward frontier AI labs unsettled at a moment of acute security stakes.

title: Tencent starts testing the new Xiaowei AI assistant powered by WeLM and DeepSeek models in its WeChat app in China, in an effort to catch up in the AI race (Luz Ding/Bloomberg)
blurb: Tencent began testing Xiaowei, a new AI assistant built on its in-house WeLM and DeepSeek models, inside WeChat for users in China. The rollout marks Tencent's attempt to close the gap with rivals like Alibaba and ByteDance, embedding generative AI directly into the messaging app that already anchors daily life for over a billion users.

title: Memory Safe Inline Assembly
blurb: Fil-C now supports GCC and clang inline assembly syntax while preserving complete memory safety, so mistakes like a missing clobber or constraint produce a panic or illegal-instruction trap instead of a silent miscompile. The feature is pre-release and not shipped in the 0.679 release, so testing it currently requires building Fil-C from source.

title: Anthropic Sued Over Claude Max Usage Limits Far Below Advertised Caps
blurb: A proposed class action filed in the Northern District of California alleges Anthropic's Claude Max plans deliver far less usage than advertised, claiming the $200 Max 20x tier provides only six to eight times Pro usage rather than the marketed 20 times. Plaintiff Karl Khan calls Anthropic's usage disclosures a black box and seeks over $5 million for U.S. customers who bought Max plans since April 2025.

title: Japan's top five chip equipment makers posted their first-ever fall in combined China sales, down 10% in the year ended March 31, as China boosts local players (Nikkei Asia)
blurb: Japan's five largest chip equipment makers reported their first-ever decline in combined China sales, down 10% in the year ended March 31, as Chinese firms shift to domestic suppliers. The drop signals that Beijing's push to build local semiconductor tooling is starting to erode a market that had been a reliable growth engine for Tokyo Electron and its peers.

title: Free Software Foundation Europe pushes EU to force Google to allow AI uninstalls on Android
blurb: The Free Software Foundation Europe asked the European Commission to force Google to let Android users fully uninstall preloaded AI components, citing models that silently reinstall after deletion in violation of the Digital Markets Act. The FSFE also wants interoperability decoupled from Google's developer verification, opposing the Android Developer Certification program launching September 2026, which would require every developer to register with Google before their apps can run on certified devices.

title: Samsung Electronics brings ChatGPT and Codex to employees
blurb: Samsung Electronics deployed ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to its employees worldwide, in one of OpenAI's largest enterprise rollouts to date. The agreement puts conversational AI and coding assistance in the hands of staff across Samsung's global operations, spanning everything from chip design and manufacturing to consumer electronics teams.

title: Hundreds of AI-powered iOS apps found exposing credentials
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University analyzed 444 iOS apps with LLM features and found 282 exposing exploitable credentials or backend access, including authentication tokens, unauthenticated backends, and plaintext API keys. The leakage spans 13 app categories and affects popular titles, with 15% of vulnerable apps holding over 1,000 ratings and the largest carrying more than 2.3 million.

title: Sakana Fugu
blurb: Sakana AI released Fugu, an experiment in which an AI agent autonomously improved a small GPT's training recipe using AutoResearch, running 123 experiments over roughly 14 hours on a single H100. The agent's Fugu-Ultra configuration reached a best mean bits-per-byte of 0.9774, edging out three frontier-model baselines by tuning batch size, model depth, learning rates, and optimizer settings on its own.

title: Analysis: Anthropic may have talked itself into an export ban, as its 2026 official statements and posts used AI risk-related terms 8x more often than OpenAI (Clara Murray/Financial Times)
blurb: A Financial Times analysis found that Anthropic's 2026 official statements and posts referenced AI risk-related terms roughly 8x more often than OpenAI's, and argues that this emphasis may have undercut the company in policy circles. The piece suggests Anthropic's vocal risk messaging could have contributed to support for export restrictions that work against its own commercial interests.

title: Agent Beacon: Open-source telemetry layer for AI agents
blurb: Asymptote Labs released Agent Beacon, an open-source telemetry layer that configures runtimes like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Cursor to export OpenTelemetry data and writes a normalized JSON log of agent file edits, commands, and tool calls across local, CI, and cloud surfaces. Written in Go under the MIT license, it runs locally without an account, binds its dashboard to loopback, and supports configurable retention modes plus forwarding to customer-managed SIEMs.

title: Samsung rolls out ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to all staff in Korea and DX division staff globally; OpenAI says it's one of its largest enterprise deployments (Kim Su-jeong/CHOSUNBIZ)
blurb: Samsung rolled out ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to all of its staff in Korea and to Device eXperience division employees globally. OpenAI called it one of its largest enterprise deployments to date, extending the company's reach across one of the world's biggest electronics manufacturers.

title: The Doom Justifies the Valuation
blurb: George Hotz argues that AI labs lean on doom and existential-risk framing because the current technology doesn't justify their valuations. He contrasts the GLM-5.2 release post, a technical look at incremental progress on a model he rates on par with Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5, with Anthropic's posts about exponential acceleration and recursive self-improvement, which he reads as hype optimized to induce panic rather than describe a product.

title: 잘못된 Claude.md가 AI를 망친다…연구진이 꼽은 설정 파일 6대 문제
blurb: Researchers at the Federal University of Minas Gerais proposed the first catalog of "configuration smells" in coding-agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, identifying six recurring defects that bloat context, waste tokens, and degrade reliability. Across 100 popular open-source repositories, 91 contained at least one smell, with lint leakage appearing in 62%, context bloat in 42%, and skill leakage in 35%.

title: Tencent starts testing the new Xiaowei AI assistant powered by WeLM and DeepSeek models in its WeChat app in China, in an effort to catch up in the AI race (Luz Ding/Bloomberg)
blurb: Tencent began testing Xiaowei, a new AI assistant powered by its in-house WeLM and DeepSeek models, inside WeChat in China. The rollout is part of Tencent's effort to close the gap with domestic rivals in the AI race, embedding the assistant directly into the country's dominant messaging app.

title: Show HN: Recall – Local project memory for Claude Code
blurb: Recall is a local memory plugin for Claude Code that logs each session to an append-only history file and condenses it into a compact context.md summary using a classical Python summarizer rather than an LLM call. It solves the cold-start problem without spending model tokens or sending transcripts to any external API, working offline the moment the plugin loads.

title: Good results fine tuning a local LLM like Qwen 3:0.6B to categorize questions
blurb: This post tests whether a 600M-parameter local model, Qwen 3:0.6B, can be fine-tuned into a reliable question classifier for a metadata-aware RAG chatbot. Using the Unsloth framework on ~850 household questions split 70/15/15, the author categorizes queries into known buckets like pool, car, and HVAC to narrow the vector search space before retrieval.

title: AI 도입 넘어 비용 관리로…오픈AI, 사용량 분석·예산 설정 기능 공개
blurb: OpenAI added spending controls and expanded usage analytics to ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins a centralized dashboard to monitor adoption, track per-team consumption, and set AI budgets. A new Global Admin Console breaks down ChatGPT and Codex credit usage by user, product, and model, though analysts note the tools still measure activity rather than tie AI spending to actual business outcomes.

title: NSA director: 'Mythos "broke into almost all of our classified systems in hours"
blurb: The NSA director said an AI system called Mythos broke into almost all of the agency's classified systems within hours during a security evaluation. The disclosure points to the growing offensive capability of autonomous AI in cyber operations and raises questions about whether existing government defenses can withstand machine-speed intrusion attempts.

title: Anthropic Sued Over Claude Max Usage Limits Far Below Advertised Caps
blurb: A Claude subscriber filed a proposed class action against Anthropic, alleging its Max plans deliver far less usage than advertised. The complaint says the $200-a-month Max 20x tier provides only six to eight times the usage of Pro rather than the marketed 20 times, calls Anthropic's usage disclosures a black box, and seeks over $5M for U.S. Max buyers since April 2025.

title: San Diego-based Aether AI, which is building "causal world models" to teach robots cause and effect instead of pattern-matching, raised a $20M seed led by MPCi (Cristian Dina/The Next Web)
blurb: Aether AI raised a $20M seed round led by MPCi to build "causal world models" that teach robots cause and effect rather than statistical pattern-matching. The San Diego startup aims to give robots a structural understanding of how actions produce outcomes, an approach it argues generalizes better to unfamiliar physical environments than current imitation-based methods.

title: There is minimal downside to switching to open models
blurb: The author argues that switching from proprietary models like Claude and GPT to open models now carries minimal downside, drawing a parallel to Linux shedding its professional-risk reputation. While proprietary models still top intelligence leaderboards and offer cleaner APIs and stronger privacy guarantees, the post contends that gap is narrowing fast, with Claude's new ID verification rollout pushing the author to make the move for professional work.

title: Analysis: Anthropic may have talked itself into an export ban, as its 2026 official statements and posts used AI risk-related terms 8x more often than OpenAI (Clara Murray/Financial Times)
blurb: Anthropic's own messaging may be working against it. An FT analysis found the company's 2026 official statements and posts used AI risk-related terms eight times more often than OpenAI's, and that framing is now feeding the case for tighter export controls. The lesson for founders is that how you talk about your own technology becomes raw material for regulators, and a safety-forward narrative can harden into the rules you later have to operate under.

title: European Investor Seedcamp Closes On $320M Across Two Funds To Back Seed Startups And Reaches $1B AUM
blurb: Seedcamp closed two new funds totaling $320M, a $220M seventh core fund and a $100M select fund, pushing the European seed investor past $1B in assets under management since its $3M start in 2007. The firm plans 35 new investments a year at checks up to $1.3M, leading roughly 70% of deals for 5% to 10% ownership, and is steering away from crowded AI verticals toward defense, health, and robotics. Its edge is a portfolio community where founders just slightly ahead become the most useful help for the next cohort.

title: PitchBook: defense tech companies making drones, battlefield AI, and more have raised $12.3B across 175 deals YTD, up from a $9.95B total from 158 deals in 2025 (Financial Times)
blurb: Defense tech has already raised $12.3B across 175 deals this year, blowing past the $9.95B and 158 deals that the category logged across all of 2025. The money is going to drones, battlefield AI, and the rest of the hardware-heavy stack that most software investors spent the past decade avoiding. The signal for founders is that capital is now flowing into categories with long sales cycles and government buyers, which rewards a different playbook than the SaaS motion most startups were built around.

title: The Doom Justifies the Valuation
blurb: George Hotz argues that frontier labs lean on apocalyptic AI rhetoric because the technology itself no longer justifies the valuation. He contrasts GLM-5.2's plain technical writeup, a model on par with Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5, with Anthropic's posts about exponential risk and recursive self-improvement, and reads the doom framing as optimized to sustain a story of infinite future value. The claim is that hype substitutes for product when the current capability can't carry the price.

title: China adds ten US companies, including MP Materials and Teal Drones, to export controls and bars government purchases from 46 US companies, including Anduril (Wataru Suzuki/Nikkei Asia)
blurb: China barred its government from buying from 46 US companies, including defense startup Anduril, and added ten more, among them rare-earth miner MP Materials and Teal Drones, to its export control list. The move walls off China's state procurement and critical-minerals supply from a widening set of US defense and hardware firms. For founders building in defense tech, drones, or anything touching rare earths, the China market and its inputs are increasingly off the table, and that constraint now has to be priced into where you source and who you sell to.

title: San Diego-based Aether AI, which is building "causal world models" to teach robots cause and effect instead of pattern-matching, raised a $20M seed led by MPCi (Cristian Dina/The Next Web)
blurb: Aether AI raised a $20M seed led by MPCi to build "causal world models" that teach robots cause and effect instead of pattern-matching.

title: Seedcamp, Europe’s original ‘first cheque’ investor, raises $320m
blurb: Seedcamp raised $320M for its seventh fund, splitting $220M into its flagship first-cheque vehicle and $100M into a Select fund that follows winners toward Series B. The firm that wrote Europe's earliest checks into Revolut, Wise, and Synthesia now manages over $1B and is pairing its early-stage book with an expanded US team to pull portfolio companies transatlantic from day one. The bet for Fund VII is AI and hard tech with applications in science and the physical world, signaled by recent deals in space manufacturing, autonomous robotics, and AI agents.

title: COMAND AI lève 32 millions d’euros : la défense européenne ne vise plus la souveraineté, mais la suprématie
blurb: Comand AI raised €32M in a Series A led by Blossom Capital, with Saab taking a strategic stake, to scale Prevail, its AI command-and-control platform already deployed with units in France, Germany, and Ukraine. The round signals a shift in how European defense founders pitch: the goal is no longer a sovereign alternative to US software but setting the global standard for military command systems. Alongside Helsing, Quantum Systems, and Tekever, this cohort is reframing European defense from strategic autonomy to industrial supremacy, with Ukraine as the proving ground that changed investor appetite.

title: Deno Desktop
blurb: Deno v2.9.0 introduces `deno desktop`, a command that turns any Deno project from a single TypeScript file to a Next.js, SvelteKit, or Vite SSR app into a self-contained, cross-compilable binary bundling the runtime and a web rendering engine. It defaults to the OS webview for small binaries with optional bundled Chromium, swaps socket IPC for in-process bindings, and ships built-in bsdiff auto-update, though it currently runs only in the canary build.

title: Hundreds of AI-powered iOS apps found exposing credentials
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University analyzed 444 iOS apps with LLM features and found 282, or 64%, exposed exploitable credentials, including 54 plaintext API keys and 136 leaked authentication tokens. The most popular affected app had over 2.3 million ratings, and 55% of leaks routed traffic through custom developer backends, meaning provider-side fixes alone cannot close the gap.

title: Good results fine tuning a local LLM like Qwen 3:0.6B to categorize questions
blurb: The author fine-tuned Qwen 3:0.6B with Unsloth on ~850 household questions to classify queries into metadata categories like pool, car, and HVAC, using the labels to narrow the vector search space before a RAG lookup. The post benchmarks the tuned 600M-parameter classifier against a prompt-only baseline across ~130 integration tests, walking through the 70/15/15 train/eval/test split and the accuracy gains from fine-tuning.

title: Memory Safe Inline Assembly
blurb: Fil-C, the memory-safe C dialect, now supports GCC/clang inline assembly syntax in a pre-release build, preserving the exact assembly template you write while guaranteeing memory safety. The post details how mistakes that would silently miscompile in standard C, like omitting a `+` constraint or a `cc` clobber, instead degrade to a panic or an illegal instruction trap at worst.

title: 잘못된 Claude.md가 AI를 망친다…연구진이 꼽은 설정 파일 6대 문제
blurb: Researchers cataloged six "configuration smells" in Claude.md/Agents.md files, with lint leakage and context bloat the most common.

title: Show HN: Recall – Local project memory for Claude Code
blurb: Recall is a local memory plugin for Claude Code that logs each session to an append-only `history.md` and condenses it into a compact `context.md` summary using a classical Python summarizer rather than an LLM call. It solves the cold-start problem without spending model tokens or sending transcripts to any external API, so resuming a project loads from a ~1-2K token digest instead of re-explaining everything each session.

title: There is minimal downside to switching to open models
blurb: Drawing an analogy to the years when running Linux carried real professional risk, the author argues that open-weight LLMs have closed enough of the gap that switching away from Claude and GPT now costs little, a shift prompted by Claude's ID verification rollout. The remaining trade-offs are concrete: open models still trail proprietary ones on leaderboards, and self-hosting to preserve privacy is some combination of expensive, complicated, and slow.

title: John Ternus set to re-establish importance of Apple’s design team when he takes over as CEO: report
blurb: Incoming Apple CEO John Ternus plans to restore the design team's executive authority, which eroded over the Cook era after Jony Ive's exit left oversight with operations and finance leaders, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. Apple currently has no senior design role, and Ternus has reportedly spent significant time with the industrial design group ahead of his September 1 start and a fall foldable iPhone launch.

title: Hundreds of AI-powered iOS apps found exposing credentials
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University analyzed 444 iOS apps with LLM features and found 282 (64%) exposing exploitable credentials, including 136 leaking authentication tokens, 92 allowing unauthenticated backend access, and 54 hardcoding plaintext API keys. The leakage was systemic rather than niche, affecting apps with up to 2.3 million ratings, and 55% routed traffic through custom developer backends that provider-side mitigations alone cannot fix.

title: Texas Parks & Wildlife Data Breach Affects 3 Million Individuals
blurb: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach affecting roughly 3 million people who bought hunting and fishing licenses, stemming from a cyberattack on an unnamed third-party license-sales vendor. Exposed data includes email and physical addresses, phone numbers, driver's license details, and passport numbers, but not Social Security numbers, dates of birth, or financial information.

title: Ethereum Layer 2 Taiko halts block production following exploit; urges users to withdraw funds
blurb: Taiko, an Ethereum Layer 2, halted block production after an exploit and urged users to withdraw funds, with onchain security firm Blockaid attributing the likely root cause to a flaw in the Taiko bridge's source-signal proof validation. The halt freezes the rollup while the validation bug is investigated, so users with assets bridged through Taiko should exit to Ethereum mainnet before block production resumes.

title: NSA director: 'Mythos "broke into almost all of our classified systems in hours"
blurb: The NSA director told The Economist that Anthropic's Mythos model "broke into almost all of our classified systems in hours," a disclosure that surfaced amid the Trump administration's move to block the company from federal work. The account underscores how far offensive AI tooling has advanced inside government red-team exercises, even as the legal fight over Anthropic's access to classified networks remains unresolved.

title: Agent Beacon: Open-source telemetry layer for AI agents
blurb: Asymptote Labs released Beacon, an open-source (MIT, mostly Go) telemetry layer that configures OpenTelemetry export and hooks for AI coding agents like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Cursor, then normalizes their file edits, command execution, tool use, and approval decisions into a single JSON event log. It runs locally without an account, binds its dashboard to loopback, offers redaction and retention modes, and can forward events into customer-managed SIEMs.

title: Encrypted DNS still tells an eavesdropper where to look
blurb: Encrypted DNS over TLS, HTTPS, QUIC, and CoAP hides query contents but leaves plaintext headers that mark a flow as DNS, letting an eavesdropper on a wireless link single it out to block, profile, or analyze it. A new study trained a Random Forest classifier on 296 IoT scenarios and found addresses, ports, and monotonic counters leak the most. The authors propose eliding headers via SCHC compression and CoAP block-wise transfer to even out packet lengths.

title: Samsung rolls out ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to all staff in Korea and DX division staff globally; OpenAI says it's one of its largest enterprise deployments (Kim Su-jeong/CHOSUNBIZ)
blurb: Samsung deployed ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to all staff in Korea and to DX division employees globally, in what OpenAI calls one of its largest enterprise rollouts. The deal hands OpenAI a marquee reference account inside a major hardware vendor and pressures Microsoft Copilot and Google Gemini, which had been Samsung's presumed enterprise-AI defaults.

title: Samsung Electronics brings ChatGPT and Codex to employees
blurb: Samsung Electronics deployed ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to employees worldwide, making it one of OpenAI's largest enterprise rollouts to date. The deal embeds OpenAI's models and coding agent directly into a global manufacturer's workflows, expanding OpenAI's enterprise footprint against Microsoft Copilot and Google Gemini in accounts where those incumbents already hold ground.

title: Free Software Foundation Europe pushes EU to force Google to allow AI uninstalls on Android
blurb: The Free Software Foundation Europe asked the European Commission to force Google to let Android users fully uninstall pre-loaded AI models and to decouple interoperability from Google's developer-verification requirements, citing DMA violations after Google's AI components silently reinstalled when deleted. The submission targets Google's Android Developer Certification, due September 2026, which would require every developer to register, sign contracts, and pay account fees to publish apps even on alternative stores like F-Droid.

title: AI 도입 넘어 비용 관리로…오픈AI, 사용량 분석·예산 설정 기능 공개
blurb: OpenAI added spend controls and enhanced usage analytics to ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins a centralized Global Admin Console that tracks ChatGPT and Codex credit consumption by user, product, and model, plus per-team budgets. The tooling answers enterprise demand for AI cost governance as agent sprawl grows, though Forrester and Gartner analysts note it measures activity rather than tying spend to business outcomes.

title: Agent Beacon: Open-source telemetry layer for AI agents
blurb: Asymptote Labs released Beacon, an open-source Go tool that configures OpenTelemetry export for AI coding agents like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Cursor, normalizing their file edits, command runs, and tool calls into a single JSON event log readable from a loopback dashboard or forwarded to a SIEM. It runs locally under MIT license with no account required, defaulting to full capture but offering redacted retention modes that strip prompt text, command output, and raw diffs.

title: Polymarket paid creators to stage fake winning bets on dummy sites: WSJ
blurb: A Wall Street Journal investigation found that Polymarket paid influencers to record hype videos showing fake winning bets placed on dummy versions of the prediction-market site. The Journal reviewed more than 1,100 videos and concluded that none of the roughly $1.9 million in wagers displayed were real, raising fresh scrutiny of the platform's marketing as it pushes for US expansion.

title: Ethereum Layer 2 Taiko halts block production following exploit; urges users to withdraw funds
blurb: Taiko, an Ethereum Layer 2, halted block production after an exploit and urged users to withdraw funds from the network. Onchain security firm Blockaid attributed the likely root cause to a flaw in the Taiko bridge's source-signal proof validation, raising questions about cross-chain message integrity on optimistic rollups.

title: Bitcoin developers want to fix the 'replace this transaction with a higher fee' button. Here's why
blurb: Bitcoin Core developers are proposing to remove explicit replace-by-fee (RBF) signaling from wallet software, since full-RBF is now the network's default policy and the opt-in flag has become redundant. Keeping the legacy signal leaves an on-chain fingerprint that reveals which wallet sent a transaction, so developers are coordinating on a shared default sequence number, likely the already dominant MAX-2, to make transactions across wallets indistinguishable.

title: Ethereum validators asked to fund projects with up to 10% of staking rewards under new proposal
blurb: A new Ethereum governance proposal called "validator redirected revenue" would let validators redirect 0% to 10% of their staking rewards toward shared ecosystem infrastructure and public goods, with funds routed through a splitter contract. If a majority of validators signal support for a nonzero rate, the contribution becomes mandatory for all, potentially channeling tens of thousands of ETH annually into projects that address the network's free-rider problem.

title: Japan’s corporate pension fund plans to allocate 1% of assets to crypto: reports
blurb: Japan's National Business Corporate Pension Fund plans to allocate roughly 1% of its 21.3 billion yen ($131.8M) in assets to crypto starting in fiscal 2026, investing through a passive fund run by a hedge fund. The move accompanies a broader currency-diversification shift that cuts the fund's yen weighting from 80% to 70%, and lands as Japan advances a bill to classify crypto as financial instruments.

title: Bitget enables direct US stock purchases using crypto
blurb: Bitget launched Stock+, a feature that lets users buy full and fractional US stocks funded directly with crypto through regulated brokers. The product routes orders to licensed brokerages, bringing equity exposure onto the exchange without requiring users to convert holdings to fiat first.

title: Altura winds down stablecoin vault after ‘unprecedented level’ of withdrawal requests
blurb: Altura wound down its stablecoin vault after what the company called an unprecedented level of withdrawal requests, with its CEO attributing the run partly to market speculation. The exit follows contagion fears tied to the depeg of Main Street's msUSD, underscoring how quickly redemption pressure can cascade across linked stablecoin and yield-vault products.

title: South Korea’s Toss Bank to test blockchain-based financial infrastructure on Solana
blurb: Toss Bank, South Korea's internet-only bank with 15 million customers, signed an MOU with the Solana Foundation to build blockchain financial infrastructure, starting with a proof-of-concept for stablecoin-based overseas remittances and settlement. The first phase verifies technical feasibility of Solana-powered transfers before later phases add overseas partners and AML/KYC checks across Toss's existing 30-country, seven-currency remittance network.

title: Japan’s corporate pension fund plans to allocate 1% of assets to crypto: reports
blurb: Japan's National Business Corporate Pension Fund plans to allocate roughly 1% of its 21.3 billion yen ($131.8M) in assets to crypto in fiscal 2026, investing through a hedge-fund-managed passive fund. The move, framed as currency-risk diversification, lands as Japan advances a bill to classify crypto as financial instruments and its three megabanks prepare a jointly issued stablecoin.

title: Polymarket paid creators to stage fake winning bets on dummy sites: WSJ
blurb: The Wall Street Journal reported that Polymarket paid influencers to film fake winning bets, with none of the roughly $1.9 million in wagers shown across more than 1,100 reviewed videos turning out to be real. The findings raise consumer-protection and disclosure questions for prediction markets as Polymarket pushes into the regulated US market following its CFTC-cleared reentry.

title: South Korea’s Toss Bank to test blockchain-based financial infrastructure on Solana
blurb: Toss Bank, South Korea's internet-only bank serving 15 million customers, signed an MOU with the Solana Foundation to build blockchain-based financial infrastructure, starting with a stablecoin remittance proof-of-concept. The first phase tests technical feasibility for overseas transfers, with later phases adding overseas partners and KYC/AML verification, extending remittance rails that already cover 30 countries and seven currencies.

title: Bitcoin developers want to fix the 'replace this transaction with a higher fee' button. Here's why
blurb: Bitcoin Core developers are proposing to remove explicit replace-by-fee (RBF) signaling from wallet software, since full-RBF is now the network's default policy and the opt-in flag has become redundant. The legacy signal leaves an on-chain fingerprint that reveals which wallet sent a transaction, so developers are coordinating on a common default sequence number, likely the already dominant MAX-2, to make transactions across wallets harder to track.

title: Bitget enables direct US stock purchases using crypto
blurb: Bitget launched Stock+, letting users buy full and fractional US stocks funded directly from crypto balances and settled through regulated brokers. The feature folds equities into a crypto-exchange wallet without a separate brokerage transfer, extending the playbook of trading apps that have blurred the line between crypto and traditional securities access.

title: Hong Kong Seeks IPO Access for Chinese Investors, Higher Quotas
blurb: Hong Kong is in talks with mainland regulators to widen cross-border investment quotas and let Chinese investors buy into local IPOs, Financial Secretary Paul Chan said. The move follows a crackdown on illicit offshore capital flows and would channel mainland demand into Hong Kong's listing market, deepening liquidity for issuers choosing the city over New York.

title: Source: Morgan Stanley is pitching data center developers to use leveraged loans rather than bonds, and estimates ~$15B will be issued this year in such loans (Dakin Campbell/The Information)
blurb: Morgan Stanley is pitching data center developers to finance construction with leveraged loans instead of bonds, and estimates roughly $15B in such loans will be issued this year. The shift moves AI-infrastructure debt into the higher-yield loan market, exposing banks and private-credit funds to construction and lease risk on capital-intensive projects whose demand is tied to a still-unproven AI buildout.

title: Bond Traders Finally Get Single View of Activity in UK Market
blurb: The UK's bond-market consolidated tape went live, giving traders a single stream of post-trade data covering more than 95% of the market after years of regulatory groundwork. The launch closes a long-standing transparency gap between UK fixed income and equities, giving buy-side desks a clearer read on pricing and liquidity in a market long fragmented across dealers.

title: Altura winds down stablecoin vault after ‘unprecedented level’ of withdrawal requests
blurb: Altura is winding down its stablecoin vault after what the firm called an unprecedented level of withdrawal requests, with its CEO attributing the run partly to market speculation tied to the depegging of Main Street's msUSD. The episode underscores how quickly contagion spreads across stablecoin yield products, where a single depeg can trigger redemption pressure that forces otherwise solvent vaults to unwind.
